narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, we know how much effort and determination it takes to pursue this path, and we're here to help you understand the automated decision on your application in a clear way. Your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 4, a Year 3 decile of 2, lower-middle family income, an LSAT score of 24, an undergraduate GPA of 2.40, and plans to attend full-time—details that paint a picture of your unique journey so far. The admissions model, trained on data from many students' bar exam outcomes, predicted a 50.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which tipped the scale toward rejecting your application to prioritize those with stronger predicted odds of success. The most influential factor pulling in a positive direction—toward predicting you'd pass—was actually your lower-middle family income, as students from similar backgrounds sometimes outperform expectations on the bar. However, your LSAT score had the strongest push toward predicting failure, standing well below the typical score of around 37 seen in the dataset. Next in influence, your Year 3 school decile of 2 also nudged the prediction toward failure compared to the average of about 5.5, signaling room for academic growth in later years. Your undergraduate GPA offered some counterbalance by slightly favoring a pass prediction, though it was lower than the dataset average of 3.21, while your Year 1 decile and full-time status provided minor positive leans. Overall, these elements combined to make the model see higher risk in your bar exam success compared to typical admitted students, who often have higher test scores and consistent deciles closer to the middle or top of their classes. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many from similar backgrounds triumph with targeted preparation. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your academic deciles through additional coursework, and exploring resources like bar prep programs tailored for underrepresented students like yourself. You're capable of rewriting this story; reach out to admissions advisors for personalized guidance on reapplying stronger. We believe in your future in law.
